metadata.title: Differentiation Audit: Preserving the Intellectual Target

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I'm worried I'll pass my own adaptations through the five-question audit and rubber-stamp them. Take an assignment I adapted and (1) run all five audit questions, especially whether any part now does the student's thinking, and mark each pass or fail with the specific line that decides it; (2) surface any self-flag like 'assumptions may still need defining' and turn that flag into the exact place I should re-examine; (3) show me a version where the language got simpler but the analytic demand stayed identical.

props.command: I'm worried I'll pass my own adaptations through the five-question audit and rubber-stamp them. Take an assignment I adapted and (1) run all five audit questions, especially whether any part now does the student's thinking, and mark each pass or fail with the specific line that decides it; (2) surface any self-flag like 'assumptions may still need defining' and turn that flag into the exact place I should re-examine; (3) show me a version where the language got simpler but the analytic demand stayed identical.
props.segment: Differentiation Audit: Preserving the Intellectual Target

beats.narration_text: The most dangerous differentiation error is invisible. The adapted assignment looks simpler — it is. The student completes it — they do. The work looks finished — it does. But the student never did the thinking the assignment was designed to produce, because Claude simplified the task instead of the language. The teacher does not know. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, plain-language annotation assignment

props.command: Create a plain-language version of this primary source annotation assignment for students reading two grade levels below

beats.narration_text: The learning target is non-negotiable. Simpler language is the goal. Simpler intellectual demand is the failure. The five-question audit checks whether the target survived. Question one: is the learning target preserved? Simpler vocabulary for the same analytic demand — pass. Simpler vocabulary and a simpler task — fail.
beats.new_visual_element: Target preserved check — simpler language vs simpler demand

beats.narration_text: Claude will sometimes flag its own adaptation. 'The word assumptions may still need defining for students at this level.' When Claude flags, use it. That flag is a search signal pointing at the exact place where the adaptation may have shifted from language to task. The flag is not a warning to dismiss — it is a question to answer.
beats.new_visual_element: Claude's flag — use the hedged output as a search signal

beats.narration_text: Audit question three: does any part of the adapted assignment do the student's thinking for them? If the original asked students to identify the author's claim and evaluate the evidence, and the adapted version provides the claim and asks students to circle the evidence — the thinking has moved from the student to the prompt. That is not differentiation. That is substitution.
beats.new_visual_element: Audit Q3 — does any part do the student's thinking?

beats.narration_text: Five audit questions, all of them teacher-review questions that Claude cannot answer on your behalf. Is the target preserved? Is the demand equivalent? Does any part do the student's thinking? Is the adaptation offered with dignity? Are all paths leading to the same learning outcome? Fluent adaptation aligned to nobody is still the failure.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — five-question differentiation audit checklist

beats.narration_text: Run the audit on your next differentiated assignment. Paste this.

props.command: Here is the original assignment and the adapted version. Run the differentiation audit: (1) Is the learning target preserved? (2) Is the intellectual demand equivalent? (3) Does any part of the adaptation do the student's thinking for them? (4) Is the simplification in language only, or also in task? (5) Flag any place where the adaptation may have crossed from support into substitution. [paste both versions]

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Five audit questions, all of them teacher-review questions that Claude cannot answer on your behalf. Is the target preserved? Is the demand equivalent? Does any part do the student's thinking? Is the adaptation offered with dignity? Are all paths leading to the same learning outcome? Fluent adaptation aligned to nobody is still the failure. Run the audit on your next differentiated assignment. Paste this.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Differentiation Audit: Preserving the Intellectual Target] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
